text: We present a twenty-eight-year-old female with fistulizing Crohn's disease refractory to medical treatment. She was complaining of anorexia, abdominal pain, and multiple episodes of watery diarrhea for the last six months. CT enterography revealed a complex fistulizing process involving several segments of the ileum, the ascending colon, transverse duodenum, distal sigmoid, and the dome of the urinary bladder. The whole ileocolic area was diseased. And this is the colonic margin. This is the small bowel margin. The inflammatory and fistulizing mass was fistulizing to duodenum.

The surgeon identifies the base of the iliopsoas artery and the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) in the surgical field. The duodenum is involved in the fistulizing process, and the surgeon performs distal and proximal transections to minimize further opening of the duodenum beyond the fistula.

The surgeon prepares to close the surgical site using a 2-0 suture, performing an anastomosis in the colon. The duodenum is positioned as a patch to cover the repair. The visual field shows severe fistulizing disease with fistulas involving the colon and proximal bowel. A diverging ileostomy is planned for this area.

The surgeon places a purse-string suture and creates a side-to-end ileocolic anastomosis using a 31mm circular stapler. Following the resection of the sigmoid colon, a colorectal anastomosis is also performed with the same stapler.

The video shows an ileocolic resection with visible multiple anteroenteric fistulas. The transverse colon is fistulizing to the area shown, and the duodenum is also fistulizing in this region. The proximal small bowel is fistulizing, with diversion above the affected area. Multiple holes are visible, indicating iliosigmoid fistulization.
